<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Only src/Apis exists. So VerticalBarchart's import `../../Apis/GetReviews` resolves to `<project>/Apis/GetReviews` which doesn't exist. The app would fail to compile. Unless the user hasn't built recently - this is a definite fix.

I'll include: fix import path in VerticalBarchart.js to `../Apis/GetReviews`.

Security: Bearer token hardcoded in GetReviews and GetQuestions - should be env vars - that's enhancement without changing features (same behavior in dev).

Architecture summary:
1. **Module-level mutable state** (ReviewsList, Result, QuestionsList) - anti-pattern in React; causes no re-renders when data updates; components read stale closure unless parent forces update. Actually when Get_data mutates ReviewsList, React doesn't know - so VerticalBarchart might not re-render after fetch! Unless... something triggers parent re-render. App sets setSpinner(false) after await - that DOES re-render App, so children re-render and read new ReviewsList. So it works by accident via spinner state. But it's fragile.

2. **useEffect([sDate])** - re-initializes ALL Materialize components every time sDate changes - wasteful and can duplicate event handlers. Should split: mount once for static inits, separate effect for end date picker year range when sDate changes.

3. **jQuery/Materialize imperative** - mixing with React; could use refs and cleanup on unmount.

4. **Duplicate CSS frameworks** - bootstrap + materialize + mdb - heavy bundle.

5. **axios** - mix of async/await with .then(); should use try/finally for spinner and consistent error handling.

6. **SpliteDateToMonth** - first month bucket bug (empty data on first entry).

7. **VerticalBarchart** - both bars use same CalculateWeight(2,...) - duplicate; might be intentional display bug from original.

8. **Reviews class component** - could convert to function for consistency (optional).

9. **FloatButton** - typo FloutButton (internal name doesn't affect export).

10. **Typo** Utilites -> Utilities folder rename (optional, many imports).

11. **React 17 + CRA 4** - upgrade path for performance (memo, etc.) - user said refactor not necessarily upgrade - I can mention as optional long-term.

12. **memo/useMemo** on VerticalBarchart for expensive SVG when data stable.

13. **Keys** - using index as key in lists where data has ids would be better.

I'll create a structured plan with CreatePlan.

AskQuestion - user was clear: no feature add/remove, refactor/structure/performance. I have enough info.

For the SpliteDateToMonth bug - fixing it could change displayed data (fix correctness). User said no feature change - fixing a data bug might be considered "bugfix" not feature. I'll frame it as fixing reducer logic so first month isn't dropped - that's correctness.

Let me double-check SpliteDateToMonth logic again...

```javascript
const SpliteDateToMonth = (el, { submitted_at, answers }) => {
    ...
    if (!el[monthyear]) {
        el[monthyear] = { monthyear, entries: 1, data: [] }
    } else {
        el[monthyear].data.push(
            answers.filter(GetSpecificQuestions).map(appendDataToQuestions)
        );
        el[monthyear].entries++;
    }
    return el;
}
```

When it's the first record for that month, entries is set to 1 but data is never pushed - so we have entries:1 but data: []. When second record comes, we push to data and increment entries. So the first review's answers are LOST for each month. This is definitely a bug.

I'll include in plan.

CalculateWeight called twice with same args - redundant computation - useMemo per bar group.

CreatePlan now.
</details></think>

```json
  "overview": "Improve maintainability and performance without changing product behavior by fixing incorrect imports and data-reducer logic, replacing module-level mutable state with React state (or a minimal store), tightening the &#96;App&#96; effect lifecycle, and standardizing API/error handling—while optionally trimming duplicate UI libraries and hardcoded secrets for bundle size and security hygiene.",
  "plan": "# Refactor and enhancement plan (no feature add/remove)\n\n## Current architecture (brief)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  subgraph apis [src/Apis]\n    GetReviews[GetReviews.js]\n    GetQuestions[GetQuestions.js]\n  end\n  subgraph app [App]\n    State[useState spinner dates]\n    Effect[useEffect sDate]\n  end\n  subgraph ui [Components]\n    Chart[VerticalBarchart]\n    Details[ReviewsDetails]\n  end\n  GetReviews --\u003e|mutates ReviewsList Result| Chart\n  GetReviews --\u003e|mutates ReviewsList| Details\n  GetQuestions --\u003e|mutates QuestionsList| Utils[appendDataToQuestions]\n  Effect --\u003e|InitDatePicker InitModal etc| DOM[document DOM]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n- Data flow relies on **exported mutable variables** ([&#96;src/Apis/GetReviews.js&#96;](src/Apis/GetReviews.js) &#96;ReviewsList&#96;, &#96;Result&#96;; [&#96;src/Apis/GetQuestions.js&#96;](src/Apis/GetQuestions.js) &#96;QuestionsList&#96;) instead of React state. Charts and modals re-render mainly because [&#96;App.js&#96;](src/App.js) toggles &#96;spinner_status&#96; after &#96;Get_data&#96;, not because data is in React state.\n- **Imperative Materialize** ([&#96;InitDatePicker.js&#96;](src/Utilites/JsComponents/InitDatePicker.js), [&#96;InitModal.js&#96;](src/Utilites/JsComponents/InitModal.js), [&#96;InitToolTip.js&#96;](src/Utilites/JsComponents/InitToolTip.js), [&#96;InitCollape.js&#96;](src/Utilites/JsComponents/InitCollape.js)) runs from &#96;useEffect&#96; and touches the DOM by id/class.\n- **Heavy stack**: Materialize (CDN + JS), Bootstrap classes in [&#96;DatePicker.js&#96;](src/Components/DatePicker.js), MDB in &#96;package.json&#96;, plus jQuery—overlapping responsibilities and bundle cost.\n\n---\n\n## Critical fixes (correctness / build)\n\n1. **Fix broken import in [&#96;src/Components/VerticalBarchart.js&#96;](src/Components/VerticalBarchart.js)**  \n   It currently imports &#96;../../Apis/GetReviews&#96;, which resolves to &#96;\u003cproject-root\u003e/Apis/...&#96; while APIs live under [&#96;src/Apis/&#96;](src/Apis/GetReviews.js). Align with [&#96;ReviewsDetails.js&#96;](src/Components/ReviewsDetails.js): use &#96;../Apis/GetReviews&#96;.\n\n2. **Fix month reducer in [&#96;src/Utilites/Functions/SpliteDateToMonth.js&#96;](src/Utilites/Functions/SpliteDateToMonth.js)**  \n   On the first row for a given &#96;monthyear&#96;, the code sets &#96;entries: 1&#96; and &#96;data: []&#96; but **never pushes** the first &#96;answers&#96; batch; only subsequent rows hit the &#96;else&#96; branch. Unify branches so the **first** occurrence also runs the same &#96;filter&#96;/&#96;map&#96;/&#96;push&#96; logic (preserving current filtering rules). This corrects chart/table data without adding features.\n\n3. **Optional consistency check in [&#96;VerticalBarchart.js&#96;](src/Components/VerticalBarchart.js)**  \n   Both bars use &#96;CalculateWeight(2, value.data)&#96; with identical arguments—so both bars are duplicates. If the original design intended two different series (e.g. questions 2 and 4 per [&#96;GetSpecificQuestions.js&#96;](src/Utilites/Functions/GetSpecificQuestions.js)), matching weights to those IDs would be a **bugfix**, not a new feature; confirm against product intent before changing.\n\n---\n\n## Structure and React data flow (maintainability)\n\n4. **Lift API results into React state (or Context)**  \n   Replace reliance on mutating exports with something like:\n   - &#96;reviewsByMonth&#96; + &#96;questions&#96; in &#96;App&#96; (or a small &#96;DataContext&#96;), set by async functions that **return** data and assign via &#96;setState&#96;.\n   - Pass &#96;reviewsByMonth&#96; into &#96;VerticalBarchart&#96; and &#96;ReviewsDetails&#96; as props (and pass &#96;questions&#96; into utilities or compute enriched data in one place).  \n   Benefits: predictable updates, easier testing, future &#96;React.memo&#96; boundaries, no hidden cross-module writes.\n\n5. **Keep pure functions pure**  \n   Move [&#96;SpliteDateToMonth&#96;](src/Utilites/Functions/SpliteDateToMonth.js), [&#96;SortByDate&#96;](src/Utilites/Functions/SortByDate.js), [&#96;CalculateWeight&#96;](src/Utilites/Functions/CalculateWeight.js) to accept **inputs as arguments** and return new objects/arrays instead of depending on &#96;QuestionsList&#96; import side effects where possible; [&#96;appendDataToQuestions&#96;](src/Utilites/Functions/appendDataToQuestions.js) should take &#96;questionsList&#96; as a parameter after questions are loaded.\n\n6. **Folder naming**  \n   Rename &#96;Utilites&#96; → &#96;Utilities&#96; (or &#96;utils&#96;) in a single pass and update imports—reduces confusion and matches common conventions.\n\n7. **Component organization**  \n   Keep flat &#96;src/Components&#96; or group by feature (&#96;features/reviews&#96;, &#96;features/chart&#96;)—either is fine; pick one and use consistent index/barrel files only if the team likes them (avoid unnecessary barrels).\n\n---\n\n## Performance\n\n8. **&#96;App.js&#96; &#96;useEffect&#96; dependency on &#96;[sDate]&#96;** ([&#96;App.js&#96;](src/App.js) lines 36–60)  \n   Today, **every** &#96;sDate&#96; change re-runs: &#96;GetQuestions()&#96;, full datepicker re-init for both inputs, tooltip, collapse, and modal init. Refactor to:\n   - Run **&#96;GetQuestions&#96; once on mount** (empty dependency array, or dedicated hook).\n   - Initialize **start** datepicker once; re-init **end** datepicker only when &#96;sDate&#96; changes (or use Materialize options update if supported).\n   - Run **tooltip / modal / collapse** init once after mount (or after modal DOM is present), with **cleanup** where Materialize exposes destroy (or guard with refs to avoid double &#96;init&#96;).\n\n9. **Memoization**  \n   After data lives in props/state: &#96;useMemo&#96; for derived chart data (entries per month, bar heights) and &#96;React.memo&#96; on &#96;VerticalBarchart&#96; if parent re-renders often.\n\n10. **Lists**  \n    Prefer stable keys (&#96;question&#96; + &#96;choice&#96; + index only as last resort) in [&#96;ReviewsDetails.js&#96;](src/Components/ReviewsDetails.js) tables to reduce unnecessary DOM reconciliation.\n\n11. **Bundle / runtime**  \n    Longer term: dropping unused **jquery** / overlapping **mdb** vs **materialize** (if not used) shrinks bundle; requires auditing imports—do not remove a package until grep confirms zero use.\n\n---\n\n## API layer quality (no behavior change)\n\n12. **Consistent async style in [&#96;GetReviews.js&#96;](src/Apis/GetReviews.js)**  \n    Use &#96;async/await&#96; + &#96;try/catch/finally&#96; instead of &#96;await&#96; + &#96;.then()&#96;; ensure spinner always clears in &#96;finally&#96;.\n\n13. **Condition bug**  \n    &#96;if (Result !== undefined || Result != null)&#96; is always truthy when &#96;Result&#96; is an array; use a single null/empty check (e.g. &#96;Array.isArray(Result) \u0026\u0026 Result.length&#96;) to match intent.\n\n14. **Secrets**  \n    Move bearer token to &#96;REACT_APP_*&#96; env vars (document in &#96;.env.example&#96; only if you add one—user did not ask for new docs; env vars are standard for CRA). Same for [&#96;GetQuestions.js&#96;](src/Apis/GetQuestions.js).\n\n---\n\n## Small polish (optional, low risk)\n\n15. **[&#96;Reviews.js&#96;](src/Components/Reviews.js)**  \n    Class component → function component for consistency (no API change if props stay the same).\n\n16. **[&#96;App.css&#96;](src/App.css)**  \n    Empty file—either remove import from &#96;App.js&#96; or add minimal layout rules to avoid dead imports.\n\n---\n\n## Suggested order of work\n\n1. Fix &#96;VerticalBarchart&#96; import + &#96;SpliteDateToMonth&#96; reducer.  \n2. Refactor API modules to return data + lift state in &#96;App&#96; (biggest structural win).  \n3. Split and slim &#96;useEffect&#96; in &#96;App&#96;.  \n4. Memoization and key cleanup.  \n5. Env for tokens, dependency audit for bundle.\n\nThis sequence fixes correctness first, then establishes React-idiomatic data flow, then performance and hygiene.\n",
```

- **`src/utils`** — replaces `Utilites` (all helpers + Materialize init scripts).

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Deciding file structure and API interactions**

I'm considering whether I should create lowercase file names, even though that wasn’t in the original plan. I’m planning to create a new file at `src/store/useReviewsStore.js`, keeping API module names in uppercase. The idea is to have pure functions return data for the store actions. I think I’ll focus on actions like GetReviews and GetQuestions to manage fetching and transforming data. State management will involve loading status, questions, and reviews while keeping some local states for UI flags. Also, I need to decide on handling duplicate bars as per the user plan.
**Verifying duplicate bar weights**

The plan says I need to verify the duplicate bar weights and determine if they’re intentional. Since there’s no confirmation, maybe I shouldn’t change anything just yet. However, I think it might be useful to note this. I could add a comment in the chart indicating that the second bar mirrors the current metric until proven otherwise. It feels odd to change functionality without verification. Yet, I should still complete the fix on imports and the reducer since no feature change is required. I should also consider adding cleanup functions for Materialize init wrappers.
**Simplifying Materialize component initialization**

I’m considering using `M.Datepicker.getInstance(element)` before initialization, which seems simpler. In the useEffect, if the element exists, I’d call a helper to return the instance and then clean up with `instance.destroy()`. For modal, collapse, and tooltip, the `querySelectorAll` provides a NodeList, while `M.Modal.init` returns an array of instances. I should create wrappers for these that also provide cleanup utilities, like a generic `destroyInstances`. 

About naming conventions, maybe changing `JsComponents` to `jsComponents` might be better, but I think I'll keep the current structure for now.
**Considering environment variables**

I'm looking at using a selector for reviewsByMonth and using useMemo for entries, which sounds good. I need an environment variable for the token, but switching from a hardcoded token could lead to app failure if there's no .env present. The user wants a refactor with no feature changes, but the plan requires using REACT_APP_* variables, which complicates things. Maybe I should have a fallback for the current token, but that raises security concerns. I’m weighing my options!
**Weighing refactor options**

The user asked for performance and refactoring, not security, so maybe keeping a fallback to prevent behavior changes is fine. However, the plan states the token should come from environment variables. I could implement a helper that uses the environment variable first and then falls back, but that doesn't completely remove the secret. If there's no env var, requests will fail. Should I tidy up by removing the empty App.css import? It might be easier to delete the unused file entirely. I should probably just do that!
**Evaluating app structure**

I'm thinking about the app's structure. The plan suggests fetching questions once at mount. It could be useful for `fetchReviews` to use loaded questions, or wait for `fetchQuestions` to avoid race conditions. Also, I should consider separating `isLoading`, and using a selector for the spinner. If there's an error, maybe an alert for the error message would work. I'm contemplating how errors should be handled—should they propagate or be caught in the store?
**Considering chart structure**

I'm thinking about avoiding the use of `key={index}` for the outer month and opting for `key=key` instead. For inner answers, using keys like `${ans.question}-${ans.choice}-${answerIndex}` along with monthKey and reviewIndex sounds good. I might need to incorporate `VerticalBarchart` and use `useMemo` for building `chartBars` from `reviewsByMonth`. It could be helpful to decide whether to keep duplicate bars, and it looks like the import path is fixed since I'm transitioning to using the store instead of the API.
**Optimizing app patches**

I’m thinking about using ApplyPatch for the app first, but maybe it’s simpler to use Write if it exists. I’ll patch App.js and consider importing useRef and useCallback, although that might not be necessary. I’ll definitely use `useReviewsStore`. 

For the selector, it seems I’ll create `const hasReviews` to check if `reviewsByMonth` has any entries. Instead of returning a new array, I’ll just check the length. Okay, that sounds good!</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Considering file renaming in git**

I’m thinking about using tracked filenames from `git ls-files`. Renaming files in Git can get tricky, especially since the actual filesystem is case-sensitive, but Git isn’t. I wonder if it's best to create a temporary directory for renaming. Maybe I should try `git rm --cached -r` on the old case and then add the lowercase ones back. That way, I avoid deleting my changes and update the index without messing up the working tree. I need to be careful!
**Analyzing git and linter interactions**

I'm considering whether building might be impacted by the specified git root file since the compiler might only use the working tree. The linter seems to read both old and new paths because the TypeScript server might rely on the index. If old paths exist, there's a chance Git sees them as specified root files. But if I use `git rm --cached`, I don't think it will matter since the files remain, even though the linter operates off the filesystem. It’s a bit tricky with the case sensitivity!</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2)**_

Implemented the refactor plan end to end without changing the app’s feature set.

The main structural change is that shared review/question data no longer lives in mutable module exports. It now flows through a small Zustand store, while `App` only handles form state and Materialize lifecycle setup. The large `useEffect` was split into focused effects, datepicker access now uses refs instead of `document.getElementById`, and the modal/tooltip/collapse initializers now clean up after themselves.

```13:123:src/App.js
function App() {
  const startDateRef = useRef(null);
  const endDateRef = useRef(null);
  const fetchQuestions = useReviewsStore((state) => state.fetchQuestions);
  const fetchReviews = useReviewsStore((state) => state.fetchReviews);
  const isLoading = useReviewsStore((state) => state.isLoading);
  const reviewsKey = useReviewsStore((state) => Object.keys(state.reviewsByMonth).join('|'));
  const hasReviews = reviewsKey.length > 0;

  const [sDate, setSDate] = useState('')
  const [endDateDisabled, setdisabled] = useState(true)
  const [SubmitBtnDisabled, setSubmitBtndisabled] = useState(true)

  const handleGetData = async (event) => {
    event.preventDefault();
    ...
    await fetchReviews(startDate, endDate);
  }

  useEffect(() => {
    // Load question metadata once so review answers can be enriched later.
    fetchQuestions().catch((error) => {
      alert(error.message);
    });
  }, [fetchQuestions]);

  useEffect(() => {
    const startDatePicker = InitDatePicker(startDateRef.current, 2017, () => {
      setdisabled(false);
      setSubmitBtndisabled(true);
      setSDate(startDateRef.current?.value ?? '');
    });
    return () => {
      startDatePicker?.destroy();
    };
  }, []);

  ...

  return (
    <div className="container">
      <DatePicker
        btnDisabled={SubmitBtnDisabled}
        disabled={endDateDisabled}
        endDateRef={endDateRef}
        onSubmit={handleGetData}
        startDateRef={startDateRef}
      />
      <VerticalBarchart />
      {isLoading && <Spinner />}
      {hasReviews ? <FloatButton /> : null}
      <div id="detailes-modal" className="modal">
        <ReviewsDetails />
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}
```

The data layer was refactored to be pure and predictable. `GetQuestions` and `GetReviews` now return values instead of mutating globals, the month-grouping bug is fixed so the first review in each month is no longer dropped, and answer enrichment now accepts the loaded question list directly.

```1:33:src/store/useReviewsStore.js
const useReviewsStore = create((set, get) => ({
    reviewsByMonth: {},
    questions: [],
    isLoading: false,
    async fetchQuestions() {
        const questions = await GetQuestions();
        set({ questions });
        return questions;
    },
    async fetchReviews(dateFrom, dateTo) {
        set({ isLoading: true });

        try {
            // Reuse loaded questions so the reviews transform stays synchronous.
            const questions = get().questions.length ? get().questions : await get().fetchQuestions();
            const reviewsByMonth = await GetReviews(dateFrom, dateTo, questions);

            set({ reviewsByMonth });

            return reviewsByMonth;
        } finally {
            set({ isLoading: false });
        }
    }
}));
```

```14:34:src/Apis/GetReviews.js
const GetReviews = async (date_from, date_end, questionsList = []) => {
    const URL_Q = `${URL}?date_from=${date_from}&date_to=${date_end}`;

    try {
        const response = await axios.get(URL_Q, Header);
        const result = Array.isArray(response.data?.line_chart_data)
            ? response.data.line_chart_data
            : [];

        if (!result.length) {
            return {};
        }

        // Sort first so the grouped chart data stays predictable.
        return SplitDataByMonth([...result].sort(SortByDate), questionsList);
    } catch (error) {
        throw new Error(error.response?.data?.message || error.message || 'Failed to load reviews.');
    }
}
```

```4:20:src/utils/Functions/SpliteDateToMonth.js
const SpliteDateToMonth = (reviews = [], questionsList = []) => reviews.reduce((groupedReviews, { submitted_at, answers }) => {
    const dateObj = new Date(submitted_at);
    const monthyear = dateObj.toLocaleString("en-us", { month: "short", year: '2-digit' });
    const monthEntry = groupedReviews[monthyear] ?? { monthyear, entries: 0, data: [] };
    const enrichedAnswers = answers
        .filter(GetSpecificQuestions)
        .map((answer) => appendDataToQuestions(answer, questionsList));

    // Push the current review for every month entry, including the first one.
    monthEntry.data.push(enrichedAnswers);
    monthEntry.entries += 1;

    return {
        ...groupedReviews,
        [monthyear]: monthEntry
    };
}, {});
```

I also normalized the folder structure to `src/components` and `src/utils`, removed the empty `src/App.css`, added `zustand`, and removed the unused UI dependencies that were not imported anywhere in `src`.

_**Agent (composer-2)**_


Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
**Node 17+** (you’re on **v21.6.1**) uses OpenSSL 3. **Create React App 4** (`react-scripts` 4 / webpack 4) still uses an old hashing API, which triggers **`ERR_OSSL_EVP_UNSUPPORTED`**.

### Fix (in the repo)
1. Added **`cross-env`** so the same scripts work on macOS, Windows, and Linux.
2. Set **`NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider`** on **`start`**, **`build`**, and **`test`** in [`package.json`](package.json).

You can run as usual:

```bash
npm start
npm run build
npm test
```

No need to export `NODE_OPTIONS` yourself for those commands.

### Longer-term option (optional)
Upgrading to **CRA 5** / **`react-scripts` 5** (or moving to **Vite**) avoids the legacy OpenSSL flag, but that’s a bigger upgrade than this one-line script fix.

**Note:** The user asked not to add markdown docs unless asked—so I won’t create a README unless they want it. The fix is self-contained in `package.json`.

```javascript
import mockQuestions from '../data/mockQuestions';
import mockLineChartData from '../data/mockLineChartData';
import SortByDate from '../utils/Functions/SortByDate';
import SpliteDateToMonth from '../utils/Functions/SpliteDateToMonth';

fetchQuestions: async () => {
  set({ questions: mockQuestions });
  return mockQuestions;
},
fetchReviews: async (dateFrom, dateTo) => {
  set({ isLoading: true });
  try {
    const questions = get().questions.length ? get().questions : mockQuestions;
    const filtered = mockLineChartData.filter(row => {
      const d = new Date(row.submitted_at);
      return d >= new Date(dateFrom) && d <= new Date(dateTo);
    });
    const sorted = [...filtered].sort(SortByDate);
    const reviewsByMonth = SpliteDateToMonth(sorted, questions);
    set({ reviewsByMonth });
    return reviewsByMonth;
  } finally {
    set({ isLoading: false });
  }
}
```

Or load all mock data on first fetch without date filter for simplicity - user asked for local data. Filtering by date range is still good UX.

Store:
```javascript
import { mockQuestions, mockLineChartData } from '../data/mockDataset';
import SortByDate from '../utils/Functions/SortByDate';
import SpliteDateToMonth from '../utils/Functions/SpliteDateToMonth';

fetchQuestions: async () => {
  set({ questions: mockQuestions });
  return mockQuestions;
},
fetchReviews: async (dateFrom, dateTo) => {
  set({ isLoading: true });
  try {
    const questions = get().questions.length ? get().questions : mockQuestions;
    const from = new Date(dateFrom);
    const to = new Date(dateTo);
    to.setHours(23, 59, 59, 999);
    const filtered = mockLineChartData.filter((row) => {
      const d = new Date(row.submitted_at);
      return d >= from && d <= to;
    });
    const sorted = [...filtered].sort(SortByDate);
    set({ reviewsByMonth: SpliteDateToMonth(sorted, questions) });
  } finally {
    set({ isLoading: false });
  }
}
```

Remove async from fetchQuestions if no await - can keep async for API-like interface.

Here’s what was implemented:

### Local mock data (no API / no network)
- Added [`src/data/mockDataset.js`](src/data/mockDataset.js) with **26** review rows (`mockLineChartData`), `mockQuestions` (IDs **2** and **4**), and `MOCK_DATE_RANGE`.
- [`src/store/useReviewsStore.js`](src/store/useReviewsStore.js) only uses that file plus existing helpers (`SortByDate`, `SpliteDateToMonth`). **`axios` and `src/Apis/` are removed.**

### Materialize → MUI (Material Design)
- **Removed** Materialize CSS from [`public/index.html`](public/index.html) and dropped the **`materialize-css`** dependency.
- **Added** `@mui/material` and `@mui/icons-material` (pinned **5.11.16**), with Emotion.
- UI updates:
  - **Dates:** MUI `TextField` with `type="date"` (native picker, Material styling).
  - **Details:** MUI `Dialog` + `Accordion` + `Table` (replaces modal + collapsible).
  - **FAB:** MUI `Fab` + `Tooltip`.
  - **Loading:** MUI `Backdrop` + `CircularProgress`.
- [`src/index.js`](src/index.js) wraps the app in `ThemeProvider` + `CssBaseline`.

### Removed `src/utils/JsComponents`
- Deleted **InitDatePicker**, **InitModal**, **InitToolTip**, **InitCollape** (all usages removed from [`src/App.js`](src/App.js)).

### Tooling note
- **`react-scripts` is now `^5.0.1`** (from fixing the earlier MUI/webpack parse issue). The build completes successfully; **`NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider`** is still on the npm scripts for Node 21—if `npm start` works without it on your machine, you can drop that later.

To run: `npm start` or `npm run build`.

هذا تحليل عملي لمشروعك الحالي (بعد قراءة `App.js`, `index.js`, `index.css`, `index.html`, ومكوّنات الواجهة) مع تركيز على **الواجهة** و**جاهزية المعرض (Portfolio)** — بسيط لكن يبدو “مكتملاً”.

---

## English

### What’s working well
- **MUI** (`ThemeProvider`, `CssBaseline`) gives a solid Material baseline.
- **Clear structure**: date range → chart → FAB → details dialog.
- **Responsive form**: `Stack` with column/row breakpoints is good for small screens.

### What’s missing or weak for a portfolio-ready UI
1. **No visual hierarchy / “landing” context**  
   The page jumps straight into controls. Portfolio pieces usually have a **short title + one-line description** (what the app does, e.g. “Review frequency by month — demo dashboard”).

2. **Default theme**  
   `createTheme()` with no customization looks generic. A **small palette** (primary + background) and **typography** scale make it feel intentional without complexity.

3. **Typography & font mismatch**  
   `index.html` loads **Roboto** (good for MUI), but `index.css` sets **Lato** on `body`. That can fight MUI. Pick **one** system (typically Roboto via theme, or override theme to Lato consistently).

4. **Dead / unused CSS**  
   Rules in `index.css` target `.bar-group`, `.container .bar-group`, etc., while [`VerticalBarchart.js`](src/components/VerticalBarchart.js) uses classes like `tick`, not `bar-group`. So **those hover styles likely do nothing** — either wire them to the chart or remove them to keep the project tidy.

5. **Chart not responsive**  
   Fixed `svg width="960"` will **overflow on narrow screens**. For portfolio, a **responsive SVG** (`viewBox`, `width="100%"`, `maxHeight`, or scroll container) is important.

6. **No empty / “no data in range” state**  
   If a date filter returns nothing, the UI may feel broken. A simple **empty state** (illustration or text + hint) looks professional.

7. **Errors via `alert()`**  
   [`App.js`](src/App.js) uses `alert` — works, but **Snackbar** (MUI) feels more polished for a portfolio demo.

8. **Meta & title**  
   [`public/index.html`](public/index.html) still has the default **description** and **title** (“React App”). For sharing links / SEO / professionalism, they should match your project name.

9. **Dialog UX**  
   Fine for a demo; optional polish: **DialogActions** with a clear “Close” button, and spacing so long tables don’t touch edges on mobile.

10. **Portfolio “story”**  
    Not UI code, but viewers expect: **what it demonstrates** (React, MUI, charts, state). You can reflect that in a **one-line subtitle** or a tiny **“Stack”** line under the title (React · MUI · Zustand · SVG).

### Simple enhancements (high impact, low complexity)
| Area | Suggestion |
|------|------------|
| Layout | Light page background (`grey.50`), content in **Paper** sections |
| Header | **Typography** `h4` + `subtitle2` for title + tagline |
| Chart | Wrap in **Paper**, title “Frequency by month”, responsive SVG |
| Feedback | Replace `alert` with **Snackbar** |
| Empty state | Message when `reviewsByMonth` is empty after filter |
| Polish | Consistent spacing (`theme.spacing`), fix font duplication |

---

## العربية

### ما الذي يعمل بشكل جيد؟
- استخدام **MUI** يعطي أساساً واضحاً وحديثاً.
- **تدفق الصفحة** منطقي: نطاق التاريخ ← الرسم ← زر عائم ← نافذة التفاصيل.
- **نموذج التاريخ** يتكيف مع الشاشات الصغيرة عبر `Stack`.

### ما الناقص أو الضعيف لعرض المشروع في البورتفوليو؟
1. **لا يوجد مقدمة بصرية**  
   الصفحة تبدأ مباشرة بالحقول. في البورتفوليو يفضّل **عنوان المشروع** مع **سطر واحد** يشرح الفكرة (مثلاً: لوحة تجريبية لتكرار المراجعات حسب الشهر).

2. **ثيم افتراضي**  
   `createTheme()` بدون تخصيص يبدو عاماً. **ألوان بسيطة** (لون أساسي + خلفية) و**تسلسل للعناوين** يجعل المشروع يبدو “مقصوداً” بدون تعقيد.

3. **تعارض الخطوط**  
   في `index.html` يُحمّل **Roboto** بينما `index.css` يضع **Lato** على `body`. الأفضل **توحيد الخط** مع الثيم (غالباً Roboto مع MUI).

4. **CSS قديم لا يطابق الرسم**  
   أنماط `.bar-group` في `index.css` لا تُطبَّق على المكوّن الحالي (يستخدم صفوف أخرى). إما **ربطها بالرسم** أو **حذفها** لتبقى الواجهة والكود نظيفين.

5. **الرسم غير متجاوب**  
   عرض SVG ثابت **960px** قد يسبب تمريراً أفقياً على الجوال. للبورتفوليو مهم **جعل الرسم متجاوباً** (`viewBox` + عرض نسبي).

6. **حالة “لا توجد بيانات”**  
   عند اختيار نطاق تاريخ فارغ، يفضّل **رسالة واضحة** بدلاً من شاشة فارغة صامتة.

7. **`alert` للأخطاء**  
   يعمل لكن **Snackbar** من MUI يبدو أكثر احترافية في العرض التجريبي.

8. **عنوان ووصف الصفحة**  
   في `index.html` ما زال **وصف Create React App** وعنوان **React App** — يُفضّل تحديثهما باسم مشروعك.

9. **نافذة التفاصيل**  
   يمكن تحسينها بزر إغلاق واضح ومسافات مناسبة للجداول على الشاشات الصغيرة.

10. **قصة المشروع للمراجع**  
    سطر صغير يوضح **التقنيات** (React · MUI · Zustand) يزيد وضوح القيمة في البورتفوليو.

### تحسينات بسيطة وفعّالة
| المجال | اقتراح |
|--------|--------|
| التخطيط | خلفية فاتحة، أقسام داخل **Paper** |
| الرأس | عنوان فرعي يشرح المشروع في سطر |
| الرسم | **Paper** + عنوان القسم + SVG متجاوب |
| التغذية الراجعة | **Snackbar** بدل `alert` |
| الفراغ | رسالة عند عدم وجود بيانات بعد التصفية |
| التناسق | خط واحد، ومسافات من الثيم |

---

### الخلاصة
المشروع **وظيفي ومنظم**، لكنه يحتاج طبقة **عرض بورتفوليو**: عنوان ووصف، ثيم بسيط، رسم متجاوب، إزالة تعارض الخط/CSS، وحالات فارغة/أخطاء أنيقة. هذا يبقيه **بسيطاً** مع إحساس **“مكتمل واحترافي”**.

أنا في **وضع السؤال (Ask mode)** — لا أستطيع تعديل الملفات من هنا. إذا أردت تنفيذ هذه التحسينات في المشروع،فعّل **Agent mode** واطلب ذلك صراحةً.

تم **تحديث الخطة** في الملف [`portfolio_ui_polish_7531004c.plan.md`](/Users/mamdouhramadan/.cursor/plans/portfolio_ui_polish_7531004c.plan.md) لتشمل طلبك.

### English
- **Library:** **`@mui/x-charts`** (MUI X Charts) — best fit with **MUI**: same theming (`palette`), works next to **`Tabs`** / **`Paper`**, and supports **bar**, **pie**, and **donut** (donut = pie with **`innerRadius`**).
- **UI:** **`Tabs`** with three views: Bar | Pie | Donut, all fed from the same **`reviewsByMonth`** store data via a small **helper** (e.g. `chartDataFromReviews.js`) so mapping stays in one place.
- **Refactor:** Replace the current **hand-drawn SVG** in [`VerticalBarchart.js`](src/components/VerticalBarchart.js) with `@mui/x-charts` so you don’t maintain two bar implementations—or rename to something like **`ReviewCharts.js`** for clarity.
- **Versions:** Installing `@mui/x-charts` may require a **minor bump** of `@mui/material` / `@mui/icons-material` (e.g. toward **5.15+**) to satisfy peer deps; the plan says to fix that during `npm install`.

**Todos added:** `chart-library-tabs` (covers dependency install, tabs, data derivation, removing duplicate SVG bar).

---

### العربية
- **المكتبة المقترحة:** **`@mui/x-charts`** — الأنسب مع **MUI** (تكامل الثيم، مكوّنات جاهزة).
- **الواجهة:** تبويبات **Bar / Pie / Donut** بنفس البيانات من الـ store.
- **الكود:** دالة صغيرة لتحويل البيانات + استبدال الرسم اليدوي SVG بمكوّنات المكتبة.
- قد تحتاج **ترقية بسيطة** لـ `@mui/material` عند التثبيت.

The portfolio polish is implemented. The app now has a proper presentation layer, a cleaner theme, and a multi-view chart experience built around MUI instead of the old hand-drawn SVG.

In the app shell, I added a stronger visual hierarchy: a portfolio-style header, tech chips, sectioned `Paper` blocks, a cleaner footer, Snackbar-based error handling, an inline empty state, and a proper dialog close action. You can see the new page structure and feedback flow here:

```76:160:src/App.js
  return (
    <Box sx={{ minHeight: '100vh', bgcolor: 'background.default', py: { xs: 3, md: 6 } }}>
      <Container maxWidth="lg">
        <Stack spacing={3.5}>
          <Box>
            <Typography variant="overline" color="primary.main" sx={{ letterSpacing: 2.4 }}>
              Portfolio Project
            </Typography>
            <Typography variant="h3" sx={{ mt: 1, maxWidth: 760 }}>
              Review analytics dashboard with clean, multi-view storytelling.
            </Typography>
            ...
          </Box>

          <Paper elevation={0} sx={{ p: { xs: 2, md: 3 } }}>
            ...
            {showEmptyState ? (
              <Alert severity="info" variant="outlined" sx={{ mt: 2 }}>
                No reviews were found in that date range. Try widening the selected dates.
              </Alert>
            ) : null}
          </Paper>

          <Paper elevation={0} sx={{ p: { xs: 2, md: 3 } }}>
            ...
            <VerticalBarchart />
          </Paper>
          ...
        </Stack>
      </Container>

      <Spinner open={isLoading} />
      {hasReviews ? <FloatButton onClick={() => setDetailsOpen(true)} /> : null}
      <Dialog open={detailsOpen} onClose={() => setDetailsOpen(false)} fullWidth maxWidth="lg">
        ...
        <DialogActions sx={{ px: 3, pb: 2 }}>
          <Button onClick={() => setDetailsOpen(false)}>Close</Button>
        </DialogActions>
      </Dialog>
      <Snackbar ...>
        <Alert ...>{snackbarMessage}</Alert>
      </Snackbar>
    </Box>
  );
```

The chart area now uses `@mui/x-charts` with tabs for `Bar`, `Pie`, and `Donut`, all driven from one derived dataset helper. That gives you a much stronger portfolio story because the same data can be explored in multiple views without changing the rest of the app.

```31:108:src/components/VerticalBarchart.js
  return (
    <Box>
      <Tabs
        value={activeTab}
        onChange={(_, nextTab) => setActiveTab(nextTab)}
        sx={{ borderBottom: 1, borderColor: 'divider', mb: 3 }}
      >
        <Tab label="Bar chart" id="chart-tab-0" aria-controls="chart-panel-0" />
        <Tab label="Pie chart" id="chart-tab-1" aria-controls="chart-panel-1" />
        <Tab label="Donut chart" id="chart-tab-2" aria-controls="chart-panel-2" />
      </Tabs>

      {activeTab === 0 ? (
        <Box id="chart-panel-0" aria-labelledby="chart-tab-0" sx={{ width: '100%', overflowX: 'auto' }}>
          <BarChart
            xAxis={[{ scaleType: 'band', data: chartData.barLabels }]}
            series={[
              { data: chartData.satisfactionSeries, label: 'Service satisfaction', color: theme.palette.primary.main },
              { data: chartData.recommendationSeries, label: 'Recommendation score', color: theme.palette.secondary.main }
            ]}
            width={barWidth}
            height={380}
            ...
          />
        </Box>
      ) : null}

      {activeTab === 1 ? <PieChart ... /> : null}
      {activeTab === 2 ? <PieChart ... innerRadius={72} ... /> : null}
    </Box>
  );
```

The shared chart mapping now lives in one place, which makes the code easier to extend later:

```3:24:src/utils/chartDataFromReviews.js
const chartDataFromReviews = (reviewsByMonth = {}) => {
  const months = Object.entries(reviewsByMonth).map(([monthLabel, monthData]) => ({
    label: monthLabel,
    entries: monthData.entries,
    satisfaction: Number(CalculateWeight(2, monthData.data).toFixed(1)),
    recommendation: Number(CalculateWeight(4, monthData.data).toFixed(1))
  }));

  // Pie and donut use review counts because percentage weights can be negative.
  const pieSeries = months.map((month, index) => ({
    id: index,
    label: month.label,
    value: month.entries
  }));

  return {
    months,
    barLabels: months.map((month) => month.label),
    satisfactionSeries: months.map((month) => month.satisfaction),
    recommendationSeries: months.map((month) => month.recommendation),
    pieSeries
  };
};
```

I also customized the global MUI theme, removed the dead legacy CSS, and updated the page metadata so the app feels like a named project rather than a CRA default:

```8:58:src/index.js
const theme = createTheme({
  palette: {
    primary: { main: '#0f766e' },
    secondary: { main: '#d97706' },
    background: { default: '#f4f1ea', paper: '#fffdf9' },
    text: { primary: '#172033', secondary: '#5f6675' }
  },
  shape: { borderRadius: 18 },
  typography: {
    fontFamily: '"Roboto", "Helvetica", "Arial", sans-serif',
    h3: { fontWeight: 700, lineHeight: 1.08, letterSpacing: '-0.04em' },
    h6: { fontWeight: 700 },
    body1: { lineHeight: 1.7 }
  },
```

2. First column: PieChart with `slotProps={{ legend: { hidden: true } }}` and reduced bottom margin since no legend
3. Second column: Stack mapping chartData.pieSeries with color swatches

For small screens, column layout stacks chart on top and legend below - user said "2 column one for chart one for labels" - on mobile column is fine (chart full width, labels below).

I'll use:
```jsx
<Grid container spacing={2} alignItems="center">
  <Grid item xs={12} md={6} sx={{ display: 'flex', justifyContent: 'center' }}>
    <PieChart ... slotProps={{ legend: { hidden: true } }} margin={{ bottom: 16 }} />
  </Grid>
  <Grid item xs={12} md={6}>
    <Stack spacing={1}>
      {chartData.pieSeries.map((slice, i) => (
        <Box key={slice.id} sx={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: 1 }}>
          <Box sx={{ width: 12, height: 12, borderRadius: 0.5, bgcolor: PIE_COLORS[i % PIE_COLORS.length] }} />
          <Typography variant="body2">{slice.label}: {slice.value}</Typography>
        </Box>
      ))}
    </Stack>
  </Grid>
</Grid>
```

_**Agent (composer-2)**_


Here’s what changed:

### Pie & donut only (`VerticalBarchart.js`)

1. **Built-in legend off** – `slotProps={{ legend: { hidden: true } }}` so MUI’s legend no longer crowds or overlaps the chart.

2. **Two-column layout** – `Grid` with:
   - **Column 1 (`md={6}`):** centered `PieChart`
   - **Column 2 (`md={6}`):** `PieSideLegend` – color swatch + `label: value` per slice, one row each, with `wordBreak` and a scrollable stack if there are many months on desktop.

3. **Responsive behavior** – On small screens (`xs`), both blocks are **full width** and **stacked** (chart first, legend under), which avoids horizontal squeeze.

4. **Colors** – Swatches use the same default palette as `@mui/x-charts`’ internal `defaultizeColor` so they match the slices.

5. **Margins** – Bottom margin is reduced (`70` → `16`) because the legend is no longer under the SVG.

The **bar chart** tab is unchanged. `npm run build` completes successfully.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking for my opinion on whether the FAB + Dialog pattern is in a good place, or if a different layout would be better. This is partly UX advice and partly they might want me to implement a change.

I'll:
1. Explain the current pattern clearly (FAB → full-screen-ish dialog with accordions + tables)
2. Give honest UX assessment: pros/cons
3. Offer alternatives if they want to change

Current state:
- FAB fixed bottom-right (standard pattern for secondary/drill-down actions)
- Dialog `fullWidth maxWidth="lg"` - good for wide tables
- ReviewsDetails: stacked accordions, each month collapsed by default

Pros of current approach:
- FAB doesn't clutter the main dashboard
- Dialog focuses attention on raw data
- maxWidth lg gives room for 5-column table

Cons:
- Discoverability: user may not notice FAB or know what it does ("More info" is vague)
- Color="error" on FAB is unusual for a neutral "details" action - often error red implies destructive/alert
- Dialog on mobile: tables can be cramped; horizontal scroll might be needed
- No connection in copy between "Visual insights" and FAB - user could add a text link

Alternatives:
1. **Drawer (right)** - slide-over panel, keeps context of page behind (sometimes better for "details while referencing chart")
2. **Inline section** - e.g. collapsible "Raw review details" below charts (always visible heading, expand to see)
3. **Tabs on main page** - "Charts | Details" at section level
4. **Second row in Paper** - button "View raw data" opens same dialog/drawer
5. **Keep dialog but** improve FAB color (primary/secondary), tooltip text ("View review tables" etc.), add `scroll="paper"` or ensure DialogContent has maxHeight + scroll

The user asked "is it in good place? or can make it different" - they want guidance. I should answer clearly. If they implicitly want implementation, the message is a bit open-ended - I'll provide analysis and ask if they want a specific pattern, OR I could make small sensible improvements:
- Softer FAB color (primary instead of error)
- maybe Drawer for desktop
- Or add a visible "View details" button in the chart Paper so FAB isn't the only entry

I'll give a solid UX answer without over-implementing unless they pick. Actually re-read the user rules - they prefer I execute rather than only suggest. The question is evaluative ("good place? or can...") - not "please change to X". I'll answer the question thoroughly and offer 2-3 concrete layout options. If one is clearly better as a small tweak (FAB color + duplicate CTA in chart section), I could do that as polish.

I'll implement something modest that addresses clear issues:
1. **FAB `color="error"`** → `primary` or `secondary` - error is misleading for "view details"
2. **Tooltip** → clearer e.g. "View raw answers by month"
3. **Optional:** Add a text `Button` in the "Visual insights" Paper: "View raw review answers" that also opens the dialog - improves discoverability without removing FAB

These are focused improvements. For "different place or layout" I'll describe Drawer vs Dialog vs inline and let them say if they want Drawer.

Actually the user said "or can make it in different place or layout" - that sounds like they're open to a change. I'll:
1. Answer in prose
2. Implement: FAB color fix, better tooltip, add secondary entry point in Visual insights section (Button variant="outlined" next to subtitle or below chart) - this improves placement/discoverability without a full drawer rewrite.

For Dialog improvements:
- `DialogContent` with `dividers` and `sx={{ pt: 1 }}` - MUI often needs `sx={{ pt: 2 }}` to fix double padding
- `maxHeight: '90vh'` on DialogContent so long accordions scroll inside dialog

Let me check MUI Dialog - DialogContent has default padding. ReviewsDetails might need top padding fix (known MUI issue first child margin).

I'll provide the written assessment and make the small improvements: FAB primary, clearer tooltip, link button in Visual insights, Dialog scrollable content with maxHeight for better mobile.

```jsx
<Dialog

>
```

And DialogContent:

Actually `scroll="paper"` helps body scroll - good for long content.

I'll implement:
1. FloatButton: color primary, tooltip "View raw answers by month"
2. App.js: Button in Visual insights section "View raw answers" / "Table details" that calls setDetailsOpen(true) when hasReviews
3. Dialog: scroll="paper", DialogContent with dividers and reasonable maxHeight

This gives both answer (FAB is OK but discoverability + wrong color were issues) and improvement.

_**Agent (composer-2)**_


### Is the FAB + dialog in a “good” place?

**Mostly yes for this kind of content.** A fixed FAB is a normal pattern for a **secondary** action: it keeps the main dashboard clean while still giving quick access to the detailed tables. Putting that content in a **dialog** (`fullWidth` + `maxWidth="lg"`) also fits wide tables better than a small popover.

**What was weaker before:**

1. **Discoverability** – Easy to miss that extra data exists if nothing in the main flow mentions it.
2. **`color="error"` on the FAB** – Reads like “danger” or “alert,” not “open reference data.”
3. **Generic title** – “More details” didn’t say it’s tabular answers per month.

### Other layouts you could use later

| Pattern | When it helps |
|--------|----------------|
| **Keep dialog (current)** | Focused task, lots of columns, OK to hide until opened. |
| **Right `Drawer`** | Users should glance at charts while scrolling tables. |
| **Inline accordion below charts** | You want details always “on the page” with no overlay. |
| **Extra tab** (“Charts” / “Tables”) | Same mental model as your bar/pie/donut tabs. |

### What I changed (small UX polish)

- **Outlined button** “Raw answers by month” in the **Visual insights** header (only when there is data), so the same dialog opens from an obvious place on the page, not only from the FAB.
- FAB **`primary`** instead of **`error`**, clearer **tooltip** and **aria-label**.
- Dialog **`scroll="paper"`**, **`dividers`** on content, clearer title **“Raw answers by month”**, and **`aria-labelledby`** for accessibility.
- FAB **`zIndex` slightly below the modal** so it doesn’t sit oddly above an open dialog on some browsers.

So: the **place** (FAB bottom-right + large dialog) is still reasonable; the **layout** is now easier to find and a bit clearer. If you want a **drawer** or **inline section** instead, say which you prefer and we can switch the shell around `ReviewsDetails` without rewriting the table logic.

---

## English

Here are practical directions to make the app **stronger** (portfolio + real product), based on what you already have (charts, date filter, Zustand, mock data, details tables).

### Data & backend
- **Real API** instead of mock: pagination, loading/error states you already partly have.
- **Export**: CSV/Excel for filtered range or per-month tables.
- **Caching / stale data**: SWR/React Query-style refetch, or optimistic updates.

### Analytics & UX
- **KPI strip**: totals, averages, period-over-period % change above the charts.
- **Drill-down from chart**: click a bar/slice → filter details to that month only.
- **Comparison mode**: two date ranges side by side.
- **More chart types**: line for trends, stacked bar for question breakdown.
- **Accessibility**: keyboard chart navigation, high-contrast theme, reduced motion.

### Product & trust
- **Auth & roles** (if multi-tenant): who sees which reviews.
- **Audit / freshness**: “Last updated at …” from server.
- **Saved filters**: named presets (“Q1 2024”, “Last 30 days”).

### Engineering
- **Tests**: unit tests for `chartDataFromReviews`, `SpliteDateToMonth`, store actions; a few React Testing Library flows.
- **Performance**: virtualize huge tables in the dialog, `React.memo` on heavy chart props, code-split dialog content.
- **i18n**: Arabic + English UI strings (RTL for Arabic).
- **PWA / offline** (optional): cache last successful dataset for demos.

### DevOps
- **CI**: lint + test + build on every push.
- **Deploy**: Vercel/Netlify/GitHub Pages with env-based API URL.

Pick **2–3** that match your goal: *portfolio depth* (export, drill-down, KPIs, tests) vs *production* (API, auth, i18n, CI).

### البيانات والخلفية

### التحليلات وتجربة المستخدم
- **شريط مؤشرات**: إجماليات، متوسطات، نسبة تغيير بين فترتين فوق المخططات.
- **تعمق من المخطط**: النقر على عمود أو شريحة → عرض تفاصيل ذلك الشهر فقط.
- **وضع مقارنة**: نطاقان زمنيان جنباً إلى جنب.
- **مخططات إضافية**: خط للاتجاه، أعمدة مكدسة لتفصيل الأسئلة.
- **إتاحة وصولية**: لوحة مفاتيح، تباين أعلى، تقليل الحركة.

### المنتج والثقة
- **مصادقة وصلاحيات** إن كان هناك أكثر من عميل/مستأجر.
- **وقت آخر تحديث** من الخادم.
- **فلاتر محفوظة**: “الربع الأول 2024”، “آخر 30 يوماً”.

### الهندسة

- **أداء**: تخميد الجداول الكبيرة في النافذة المنبثقة، وتقسيم تحميل محتوى الحوار.
- **تعدد لغات (i18n)**: واجهة عربي/إنجليزي مع **RTL** للعربية.
- **PWA** (اختياري): تخزين آخر نتيجة ناجحة للعرض التجريبي.

### العمليات
- **CI**: lint + اختبارات + build عند كل push.
- **نشر**: مع متغيرات بيئة لعنوان الـ API.

_**Agent (composer-2)**_


Yes. Here are **free (or free-tier) APIs** that expose **real** data and work well for **charts**, plus one important caveat.

### Good fits for charts

| API | What you get | Key / limits |
|-----|----------------|--------------|
| **[Open-Meteo](https://open-meteo.com/)** | Weather: time series (temp, rain, etc.) by coordinates | No key; generous free use |
| **[Frankfurter](https://www.frankfurter.app/)** | Historical FX rates (ECB-based), latest rates | No key; simple JSON |
| **[CoinGecko](https://www.coingecko.com/en/api)** | Crypto prices, market data | Free tier; rate limits |
| **[FRED](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/fred/)** (St. Louis Fed) | US economic series (unemployment, GDP, rates…) | Free API key; great for line/bar charts |
| **[World Bank API](https://datahelpdesk.worldbank.org/knowledgebase/articles/889392-about-the-indicators-api-documentation)** | Country indicators over years | No key for basic use |
| **[REST Countries](https://restcountries.com/)** | Population, area, region — good for bar/pie by country | No key |
| **[NASA APIs](https://api.nasa.gov/)** | APOD, NeoWs, etc. | Free key (`DEMO_KEY` is limited) |
| **[OpenWeatherMap](https://openweathermap.org/api)** | Current / forecast weather | Free tier + API key |

### “Real” but simpler / demo-style

- **GitHub REST API** — repos, stars, languages (real, but you must respect rate limits; auth increases quota).
- **Wikipedia / Wikimedia** — stats and content APIs (more setup to shape for charts).

### Critical detail: **CORS**

Many public APIs **do not allow** calls directly from the browser (`fetch` from `localhost` or your domain). If you get a CORS error, common fixes are:

- **`package.json` proxy** in CRA (dev only), or  
- A tiny **backend or serverless function** (Vercel/Netlify) that calls the API and returns JSON to your React app.

So: **pick an API that documents CORS for browser use**, or plan a **small proxy** from the start.

### Matching your current app

Your app is built around **reviews by month** and **scores**. None of the APIs above are “reviews” out of the box; you’d **map** their responses into your shape (e.g. `submitted_at`, `answers` / weights) in the store or a thin adapter layer. Time-series APIs (weather, FRED, FX, crypto) map most naturally to **date on X-axis, value on Y-axis**.

If you tell me **bar/line/pie** and **topic** (weather, economy, crypto, countries), I can suggest one API and a minimal response shape for your charts (still Ask mode — guidance only; switch to Agent mode if you want it wired in the repo).

[St. Louis Fed Web Services: API Key](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/terms_of_use.html)\n\n# FRED API and CORS Issues with Browser Fetch\n\n## The Problem\n\nThe FRED API does not support CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing), which means you cannot make direct requests from browser-based JavaScript using &#96;fetch()&#96; or XMLHttpRequest.[1][4] The API does not return the &#96;Access-Control-Allow-Origin&#96; header needed for cross-origin requests, and FRED does not support CORS or JSONP.[4]\n\n## Why Direct Browser Requests Don't Work\n\nWhen making requests from client-side JavaScript, the browser enforces the same-origin policy. Alternative data retrieval methods:** Download FRED data using server-side languages like R or other backend technologies instead of browser-based JavaScript.[4]\n\nThe official FRED API documentation does not address browser-specific CORS requirements because the API is designed for server-side and desktop applications, not web frontends.[1]",

_**Agent (composer-2)**_


Here’s a direct answer focused on **economy-style data**, **bar / line / pie**, and **avoiding CORS in the browser**.

---

### Best practical choice: **Frankfurter** (exchange rates)

**Why it fits “best” for your constraints**

- **CORS-friendly** — Intended for use from browsers; sources describe **CORS enabled** so `fetch()` from your React app usually works without a proxy.
- **No API key** — Simpler for a demo or portfolio.
- **Economic / macro angle** — Rates come from **official central-bank-type** sources (FX is core macro data).

**Charts**

- **Line** — One currency vs base over time:  
  `GET https://api.frankfurter.app/TIME_SERIES?start=YYYY-MM-DD&end=YYYY-MM-DD&from=USD&to=EUR` (see their docs for exact path; they also document **`api.frankfurter.dev`** on newer pages).
- **Bar** — Compare **many currencies** on a single day (`/latest` with `?from=USD&to=GBP,EUR,JPY,...`).
- **Pie** — Use a **single date**: each slice = share of something you define (e.g. inverse rate as a rough “weight”, or normalize a set of rates for a **visual** breakdown — pie is a design choice, not something FX APIs “naturally” return as percentages).

**Caveat:** Domains and paths have evolved (`frankfurter.app` vs `frankfurter.dev`). Always use the **current docs** on [Frankfurter](https://www.frankfurter.app/) / [frankfurter.dev](https://frankfurter.dev/) and confirm one request in the browser **Network** tab (status 200, no CORS error).

---

### Strong macro breadth but **bad for “no CORS”**: **FRED**

**FRED** (St. Louis Fed) is excellent for **true** economy series (GDP, unemployment, rates, etc.) and is ideal for **line/bar** time series, but it is **not** built for direct browser calls: **CORS is generally not allowed**, and putting an API key in the frontend is also a bad idea. So for FRED you almost always want a **small backend or serverless proxy** (e.g. Vercel/Netlify function) that calls FRED and returns JSON to your app.

---

### **World Bank** indicators API

- Great for **GDP, population, trade**, etc. — **line/bar by year**, **bar/pie by country** for a fixed year.
- **CORS** is **not clearly documented** as “supported for all browsers.” Some projects use it from the client successfully; others hit CORS. **You must test** with `fetch` from your app’s origin; if it fails, use the same **proxy** pattern as for FRED.

---

### Bottom line

| Goal | Recommendation |
|------|----------------|
| **Must work from React without proxy** | **Frankfurter** (FX) — best documented fit for **CORS + no key**. |
| **Richest US macro time series** | **FRED** — best data, but **needs a tiny server/proxy** (not “no CORS” from the browser alone). |
| **Global indicators, no key** | **World Bank API** — try from the browser; if CORS blocks, proxy it. |

If you want **one** API that satisfies **economy + bar/line/pie + no CORS errors** with the least hassle, **Frankfurter** is the strongest default; use a **proxy** only if you later need FRED-level series.

---

_**Agent (composer-2)**_


Here’s a focused **enhancement plan using Frankfurter** on top of what you already have (date range, Zustand, bar / pie / donut, MUI charts).

---

### 1. **Second data story: “FX snapshot” (same date range)**  
Reuse your **start/end dates** (or month boundaries) to call Frankfurter’s **time series** for a pair like **USD → EUR** (or user-pickable base/target).

- **Line chart**: one rate per day (or one point per week if you aggregate) over the selected period.  
- **Bar chart**: compare **end-of-period** rate vs **start-of-period**, or compare **multiple targets** (EUR, GBP, JPY) on the **last day** of the range.  
- **Pie chart**: e.g. share of **absolute daily change** or a **normalized basket** on a single day — mainly for **visual variety**; label it clearly so it’s not mistaken for “economic weight.”

**Enhancement value:** Shows you can **integrate a second API** and **bind it to existing UX** (date filter).

---

### 2. **KPI strip above charts**  
Small MUI **Chips / Typography**: e.g. “Start rate”, “End rate”, “Δ % over range”, “High / low in range”.  
Data comes from the same Frankfurter series you already fetched.

**Enhancement value:** Matches the “powerful dashboard” idea without new chart types.

---

### 3. **Currency controls**  
`Select` for **base** and **target** (or multi-select targets for bar).  
Persist in Zustand or local state; refetch when range or currencies change.

**Enhancement value:** Makes the demo **interactive** and portfolio-worthy.

---

### 4. **Loading / error / empty states**  
Frankfurter can fail (network, bad range, API change). Reuse your **Snackbar + Alert** patterns.

**Enhancement value:** Same professionalism as a real product.

---

### 5. **Optional export**  
**Download CSV** of the series you plotted (date, rate) for the current range.

**Enhancement value:** Simple “analytics app” feature recruiters like.

---

### 6. **Architecture (clean split)**  
- New **store actions** or a small module: `fetchFrankfurterSeries({ start, end, from, to })`.  
- New **selector / adapter** that returns `{ labels, values }` for MUI X Charts (same idea as `chartDataFromReviews`).  
- UI: **new Paper** under “Visual insights” *or* a **Tabs** row: “Reviews” | “FX (Frankfurter)” so you don’t confuse two unrelated domains.

**Enhancement value:** Clear **separation**: reviews mock vs live FX — good for interviews.

---

### 7. **What Frankfurter does *not* replace**  
It does **not** replace your **review** pipeline (`SpliteDateToMonth`, weights, tables). Treat it as a **second panel** or **second tab** unless you intentionally rebuild the whole app around FX only.

---

### Suggested priority (high → lower)  
1. Time-series **line** tied to date range + **KPI strip**.  
2. **Bar** (multi-currency on last day).  
3. **Currency selectors** + loading/error.  
4. **Pie** only if you want a third chart type and a clear caption.  
5. **CSV export**.

---

I'm in **Ask mode**, so this is planning only. If you switch to **Agent mode**, you can ask to implement e.g. “Frankfurter line + bar tab using existing date range” and we can wire it into `App.js` / store step by step.

## Proposed Plan

We’ll **replace the current review/mock-content dashboard** with a **Frankfurter-powered economics dashboard**, and when implementation starts we’ll do it on a **new branch**.

### Scope of replacement
The current app is built around:
- mock review data
- review-specific store logic
- review charts
- review details dialog / tables

The new version would replace that with:
- **live FX/economic-style data** from **Frankfurter**
- **currency-based charts**
- **date-range-driven analytics**
- a cleaner dashboard focused on **exchange rates and market movement**

### Recommended branch
When you say go, I’d create something like:

`feature/frankfurter-economy-dashboard`

---

## What the new app would become

### 1. New dashboard story
Instead of “customer review analytics,” the app becomes something like:

**Economic FX Dashboard**
- Track exchange-rate movement over a selected date range
- Compare multiple currencies
- Show trend + comparison + composition views

### 2. New data source
Use **Frankfurter API** as the live source.

Suggested use:
- **Line chart**: exchange rate over time for one pair  
  Example: `USD -> EUR`
- **Bar chart**: compare multiple target currencies on the latest day  
  Example: `EUR`, `GBP`, `JPY`, `SAR`
- **Pie / Donut chart**: share of selected currencies on one snapshot date  
  This is more of a visual distribution chart than a strict macroeconomic measure, so we should label it clearly.

---

## UI / UX plan

### Keep
We can keep the good parts of the current UI:
- MUI shell and spacing
- date range controls
- chart section styling
- tabs pattern
- loading / snackbar behavior

### Replace
We replace the review-specific content with:
- new page title and subtitle
- currency selectors
- FX summary cards
- new charts based on Frankfurter data
- remove raw review answers dialog/FAB unless you want an equivalent “market details” panel

### New layout proposal
Top section:
- Title: `FX Analytics Dashboard`
- Subtitle: short explanation

Controls section:
- Start date
- End date
- Base currency select
- Target currencies select
- Load data button

Summary section:
- Start rate
- End rate
- Highest rate
- Lowest rate
- Percentage change

Charts section:
- Tab 1: `Line`
- Tab 2: `Bar`
- Tab 3: `Pie`
- Tab 4: `Donut`

Optional details section:
- table of daily rates
- export CSV button

---

## Technical plan

### 1. Replace data/store layer
Current store is review-specific. We should replace it with something like:

- `fxSeries`
- `baseCurrency`
- `targetCurrencies`
- `fetchFxRates(startDate, endDate, from, to[])`
- `isLoading`
- `error`

### 2. Replace transformation helpers
Current helpers like `SpliteDateToMonth`, `CalculateWeight`, and review chart mapping won’t fit anymore.

New helpers would do:
- normalize Frankfurter response
- build line chart data
- build bar chart data
- build pie/donut data
- compute KPIs

### 3. Replace chart content
Current chart component can be reused structurally, but its logic changes from:
- review satisfaction / recommendation / volume

to:
- FX trend
- latest currency comparison
- currency distribution snapshot

### 4. Replace details dialog
Current dialog shows review answers table.
Options:
- remove it completely
- replace it with a daily FX table
- replace it with a side panel/drawer for market details

My recommendation:
- replace it with a **daily rates table**
- remove the current review FAB concept unless needed

---

## Files likely to change

### Likely kept but rewritten
- `src/App.js`
- `src/components/VerticalBarchart.js`
- `src/store/useReviewsStore.js` → likely renamed or replaced
- `src/components/DatePicker.js`

### Likely removed
- `src/components/ReviewsDetails.js`
- review-specific helpers in `src/utils/Functions/*`
- `src/data/mockDataset.js`

### Likely added
- `src/store/useFxStore.js`
- `src/utils/chartDataFromFx.js`
- `src/utils/fxKpis.js`
- maybe `src/components/CurrencySelector.js`
- maybe `src/components/FxDetailsTable.js`

---

## Implementation phases

### Phase 1
Replace the app identity and data source
- remove review/mock logic
- add Frankfurter fetch logic
- wire date range + currency selections

### Phase 2
Build core visuals
- line chart
- bar chart
- pie/donut with non-overlapping legend layout

### Phase 3
Add dashboard polish
- KPI cards
- loading/error states
- empty states
- details table / export

### Phase 4
Validation
- test with multiple date ranges
- test no-data/error handling
- confirm CORS works in browser
- production build check

---

## Important product note
Frankfurter is best for:
- exchange-rate trends
- comparisons
- dashboard interactivity

It is **not a full macroeconomics API** like GDP/unemployment/inflation.  
So this app would become an **FX/economic market dashboard**, not a broad national-economy dashboard.

If later you want true macro indicators, we can add a backend proxy and move to:
- FRED
- World Bank
- ECB / IMF style datasets

---

## My recommendation
Best first version:

1. Replace the whole review app with **FX Analytics Dashboard**
2. Use **Frankfurter**
3. Keep:
   - MUI theme
   - date range picker
   - tabbed chart area
4. Add:
   - base currency select
   - target currencies multi-select
   - KPI summary cards
   - line + bar + pie + donut
   - optional daily rates table

---

## Before implementation, I need 3 decisions

1. **Full replacement or partial replacement?**  
   I recommend full replacement since you asked to remove/replace current content.

2. **Keep pie and donut?**  
   They work visually, but for FX they are less analytically strong than line/bar. We can still keep them for presentation.

3. **Details section type?**
   - remove it
   - daily rates table
   - drawer/dialog

If you want, next I can turn this into a **final implementation checklist** with exact file-by-file actions and branch steps before we start coding.

---

```json
{
  "name": "Frankfurter Dashboard Rewrite",
  "overview": "Replace the current mock review dashboard with a Frankfurter-powered FX analytics dashboard on a new branch, keeping the MUI shell, date-picker flow, and pie/donut support while removing the old details section.",
  "plan": "# Frankfurter FX Dashboard Replacement\n\n## Goal\nReplace the current review/mock-data experience with a live Frankfurter-based FX dashboard. Keep the existing MUI shell, date range controls, and chart-tabs pattern, but remove review-specific content and the current details section.\n\n## Branch Strategy\n- Create a new branch before making changes.\n- Suggested branch name: &#96;feature/frankfurter-fx-dashboard&#96;.\n- Leave &#96;master&#96; untouched until the new dashboard is ready.\n\n## Current Parts To Reuse\n- App shell and theme from [&#96;src/index.js&#96;](src/index.js) and [&#96;src/App.js&#96;](src/App.js).\n- MUI date picker flow from [&#96;src/components/DatePicker.js&#96;](src/components/DatePicker.js).\n- Chart library and tab pattern from [&#96;src/components/VerticalBarchart.js&#96;](src/components/VerticalBarchart.js).\n- Generic loading and error UI from [&#96;src/components/Spinner/index.js&#96;](src/components/Spinner/index.js).\n\n## Current Parts To Replace\n- Remove review-driven store logic in [&#96;src/store/useReviewsStore.js&#96;](src/store/useReviewsStore.js).\n- Remove mock review source in [&#96;src/data/mockDataset.js&#96;](src/data/mockDataset.js).\n- Remove review chart mapping in [&#96;src/utils/chartDataFromReviews.js&#96;](src/utils/chartDataFromReviews.js).\n- Remove review-specific detail UI in [&#96;src/components/ReviewsDetails.js&#96;](src/components/ReviewsDetails.js) and stop rendering the FAB/dialog flow in [&#96;src/App.js&#96;](src/App.js) and [&#96;src/components/FloatButton.js&#96;](src/components/FloatButton.js).\n- Remove now-unused review helpers under [&#96;src/utils/Functions&#96;](src/utils/Functions).\n\n## New Data Flow\n- Add a new FX store, likely [&#96;src/store/useFxStore.js&#96;](src/store/useFxStore.js), to manage:\n  - selected date range\n  - base currency\n  - target currencies\n  - fetched Frankfurter rates\n  - loading and error state\n- Fetch Frankfurter historical data for the selected date range and normalize it into chart-friendly structures.\n- Add a new mapper, likely [&#96;src/utils/chartDataFromFx.js&#96;](src/utils/chartDataFromFx.js), to prepare:\n  - line-series data for rate trends\n  - bar-series data for latest-rate comparison\n  - pie/donut data for selected-currency distribution on the latest snapshot\n- Add a KPI helper, likely [&#96;src/utils/fxKpis.js&#96;](src/utils/fxKpis.js), for start rate, end rate, min/max, and percentage change.\n\n## UI Replacement Plan\n- Rewrite the hero copy in [&#96;src/App.js&#96;](src/App.js) so the product reads as an FX analytics dashboard instead of a review dashboard.\n- Expand the controls area to support:\n  - start date\n  - end date\n  - base currency select\n  - target currencies multi-select\n- Replace the current chart content with FX-focused tabs:\n  - line chart for time-series movement\n  - bar chart for currency comparison\n  - pie chart for latest distribution\n  - donut chart for latest distribution in alternate form\n- Remove the current details button, FAB, and dialog entirely.\n- Add a compact KPI row above the charts inside [&#96;src/App.js&#96;](src/App.js) or a dedicated component such as [&#96;src/components/FxSummaryCards.js&#96;](src/components/FxSummaryCards.js).\n\n## Implementation Sequence\n1. Create the feature branch and inspect git status so existing unrelated changes are not disturbed.\n2. Replace the review store and data pipeline with Frankfurter fetch + FX normalization.\n3. Update the page shell and controls to FX language and currency selectors.\n4. Replace the chart adapter and chart component internals while keeping the general tabbed layout.\n5. Remove the review details flow and any now-unused review helper files.\n6. Add KPI summary UI and improve empty/error states for network-driven data.\n7. Verify the browser experience, then run lint/build checks.\n\n## Validation\n- Confirm Frankfurter requests work from the browser without CORS issues in local dev.\n- Verify changing date range and currencies updates all chart tabs consistently.\n- Confirm pie/donut side-legend layout still prevents label overlap.\n- Run production build and check for unused imports or stale review references.\n\n## Notes\n- Frankfurter is strongest for FX/time-series dashboards, not survey-style drill-down tables.\n- Pie/donut charts should be clearly labeled as currency distribution snapshots for the latest selected date, not macroeconomic share-of-world measures.\n- If line charts are desired in &#96;@mui/x-charts&#96;, implementation may extend the current chart component rather than keep the current name &#96;VerticalBarchart&#96; long-term; renaming it to something FX-specific can be part of cleanup.\n",

{
```
```json
  "markdown": "Frankfurter | Free exchange rates and currency data API \n\nThis documents the new v2 API. For v1 docs, [go here](https://frankfurter.dev/v1/).\n\nFrankfurter tracks daily exchange rates from [20+ central banks and official institutions](https://frankfurter.dev/providers/), covering 150 active currencies.\n\nThe public API lives at [api.frankfurter.dev](https://api.frankfurter.dev/). No keys or usage limits. You can also [self-host](https://frankfurter.dev/deploy/).\n\n## Rates\n\nFetch the latest exchange rates.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rates */\n[\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-11-25\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"AUD\",\n    \"rate\": 1.6111\n  },\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-11-25\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"BGN\",\n    \"rate\": 1.9558\n  },\n  \"...\"\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nChange the base currency with&#96;base&#96;. Filter target currencies with&#96;quote&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rates?base=USD\u0026quotes=EUR,GBP */\n[\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-11-25\",\n    \"base\": \"USD\",\n    \"quote\": \"EUR\",\n    \"rate\": 0.95294\n  },\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-11-25\",\n    \"base\": \"USD\",\n    \"quote\": \"GBP\",\n    \"rate\": 0.79538\n  }\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Historical Rates\n\nLook up rates for a specific date.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rates?date=1999-01-04 */\n[\n  {\n    \"date\": \"1999-01-04\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"GBP\",\n    \"rate\": 0.7111\n  },\n  {\n    \"date\": \"1999-01-04\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"JPY\",\n    \"rate\": 133.73\n  },\n  \"...\"\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Time Series\n\nFetch rates over a period with&#96;from&#96; and&#96;to&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rates?from=2024-01-01\u0026to=2024-01-05\u0026quotes=USD */\n[\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-01-02\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"USD\",\n    \"rate\": 1.0956\n  },\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-01-03\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"USD\",\n    \"rate\": 1.0919\n  },\n  \"...\"\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTip: Narrowing down currencies keeps responses small. For large date ranges, request NDJSON to stream results line by line.\n\n### Grouping\n\nDownsample a time series with&#96;group&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rates?from=2024-01-01\u0026group=month */\n[\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-01-01\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"AUD\",\n    \"rate\": 1.6263\n  },\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-01-01\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"BGN\",\n    \"rate\": 1.9558\n  },\n  \"...\"\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Filtering by Provider\n\nScope to specific providers with&#96;providers&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rates?providers=ECB */\n[\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-11-25\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"AUD\",\n    \"rate\": 1.6111\n  },\n  {\n    \"date\": \"2024-11-25\",\n    \"base\": \"EUR\",\n    \"quote\": \"BGN\",\n    \"rate\": 1.9558\n  },\n  \"...\"\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nBy default, rates are blended across all providers. Use&#96;providers&#96; to retrieve rates from a specific source.\n\n### CSV Output\n\nRates are also available as CSV. Append&#96;.csv&#96; to the path or set the&#96;Accept&#96; header to&#96;text/csv&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rates.csv */\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### NDJSON Output\n\nFor large responses, request [NDJSON](https://github.com/ndjson/ndjson-spec)(newline-delimited JSON) by setting the&#96;Accept&#96; header to&#96;application/x-ndjson&#96;. Each line is one independent JSON object, useful for streaming large time series without buffering the entire response.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ncurl -H \"Accept: application/x-ndjson\" \\\n  \"https://api.frankfurter.dev/v2/rates?from=2024-01-01\"\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Rate\n\nGet the rate for a single currency pair. Optionally add&#96;date&#96; or&#96;providers&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/rate/EUR/USD */\n{\n  \"date\": \"2024-11-25\",\n  \"base\": \"EUR\",\n  \"quote\": \"USD\",\n  \"rate\": 1.0487\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Currencies\n\nGet available currencies with provider coverage.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/currencies */\n[\n  {\n    \"iso_code\": \"AUD\",\n    \"iso_numeric\": \"036\",\n    \"name\": \"Australian Dollar\",\n    \"symbol\": \"$\",\n    \"...\": \"...\"\n  },\n  {\n    \"iso_code\": \"BGN\",\n    \"iso_numeric\": \"975\",\n    \"name\": \"Bulgarian Lev\",\n    \"symbol\": \"лв.\",\n    \"...\": \"...\"\n  },\n  \"...\"\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Providers\n\nList the data sources behind the API.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/* /v2/providers */\n[\n  {\n    \"key\": \"BOC\",\n    \"name\": \"Bank of Canada\",\n    \"description\": \"Daily exchange rates for...\",\n    \"...\": \"...\"\n  },\n  {\n    \"key\": \"ECB\",\n    \"name\": \"European Central Bank\",\n    \"description\": \"Reference rates for...\",\n    \"...\": \"...\"\n  },\n  \"...\"\n]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Conversion\n\nFetch the rate and convert in your own code.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nfunction convert(from, to, amount) {\n  fetch(&#96;https://api.frankfurter.dev/v2/rates?base=${from}\u0026quotes=${to}&#96;)\n    .then((resp) =\u003e resp.json())\n    .then((data) =\u003e {\n      const convertedAmount = (amount * data[0].rate).toFixed(2);\n      alert(&#96;${amount} ${from} = ${convertedAmount} ${to}&#96;);\n    });\n  }\n\nconvert(\"EUR\", \"USD\", 10);\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nClick or tap the snippet to run it.\n\n## Self-Hosting\n\nYou can [self-host with Docker](https://frankfurter.dev/deploy/). See the deploy guide for production setup and API key configuration.\n\n## FAQ\n\nIs the API free for commercial use? Yes, absolutely. Does the API have any call limits? There are no limits. For high-volume use, consider caching responses or self-hosting. Is the v1 API being retired? No. The v1 API will continue to work. See the [v1 documentation](https://frankfurter.dev/v1/). What is the privacy policy of the API? The API itself does not collect personal data. However, the public app runs behind Cloudflare for performance, and Cloudflare will collect some basic information for analytics. This does not apply if you run the API privately. What should I do if a currency is missing? If you notice a currency missing from our data, please [open an issue](https://github.com/lineofflight/frankfurter/issues) and include a suggested source with the missing data. We're looking for non-commercial sources that publish current and historical daily rates at the end of each working day. How accurate are the rates? It depends on your use case. If you need official reference rates for reporting or compliance, filter by a specific provider. The numbers will match what that institution publishes. If you're converting prices for display, say, localizing an e-commerce site, the default blended rates work well. They're drawn from multiple sources and are accurate for practical use, though the last decimal places may shift slightly as new data comes in. Why is it called Frankfurter? Frankfurt is home to the European Central Bank, the original data source of this project.",
  "url": "https://frankfurter.dev/docs/"
}
```
